title: What's In Their Pockets (Modern)
description: The ordinary objects people carry — the accumulated debris of a modern life. What someone keeps on them reveals more than they'd choose to say.

entries.result: A phone with a cracked screen, a low battery, and a lock screen photo of someone who might be a sibling.
A wallet containing three cards, forty dollars in mixed bills, a receipt from a motel two states away, and a library card for a different city.
A set of keys — house, car, and one that doesn't match anything obvious — on a keychain from a place that's been closed for years.
A prescription bottle. The name on the label has been scratched off. Most of the pills are still in it.
A folded piece of paper with an address and a time written in someone else's handwriting.
A loyalty card from a sandwich shop with nine of ten punches completed. The tenth punch is punched but not stamped.
A phone in airplane mode with no apps installed except one: a map app with a single saved location, unnamed.
Three crumpled receipts, a stick of gum, and a business card for a divorce attorney that has been folded and unfolded many times.
A thumb drive with a piece of tape labeled with a date six months ago.
A parking ticket from a city the person claims they've never visited.
A child's drawing, folded in half, carried in a jacket pocket long enough that the creases are soft.
A burner phone — charged, one contact, no call history.
A small notebook with numbers and abbreviations that don't correspond to any obvious system.
A wad of cash — all small bills, no coins — totaling significantly more than someone carrying this looks like they'd have.
A grocery list in handwriting that isn't theirs, for items that suggest a household they've never mentioned.
A hotel keycard. The hotel name has been removed with a razor.
A cheap digital camera — not a phone — with a full memory card and a dead battery.
Two phones. One personal, one work. They answer questions about one of them.
A printout of a news article, highlighted in three places, with a handwritten note in the margin: 'ask about this.'
A transit pass registered to a name that might not be theirs, with a travel history of the same route every day for six months until three weeks ago.
A small first aid kit — better stocked than standard — including items that suggest a specific concern.
A ring on a chain around the neck. Old. Worn.
An old photograph, printed on paper, of four people. Three of them are smiling.
A key to a locker. Public locker, by the shape. No identifying number.
A bottle of pills prescribed to someone else, for a condition that suggests what they were dealing with.
A to-do list. Most items are checked. The last two aren't. One of them reads 'tell them.'
A folded check made out in their name for an amount that doesn't match their apparent lifestyle. Signed. Not yet deposited.
An EpiPen, expired by about six months.
A small carved figure — wood, handmade — that they touch when they think no one is watching.
A company ID badge clipped to the inside of a bag, face down. The company name is visible. They haven't mentioned working there.
A torn half of a photograph. A location is recognizable in the background. The person who was in the other half is gone.
A legal pad with the top page torn off. The impression of what was written is still readable if you hold it right.
A handwritten list of names. Some are crossed out.
A coin from a country that hasn't existed for thirty years, carried in a front pocket.
An encrypted messaging app, open to a conversation. The other party's name is a number.
A folded piece of paper with a handwritten apology on it. Never sent.
A portable charger, two charging cables, and a habit of never letting the phone die.
A bottle of expensive painkillers, full, alongside a bottle of cheap ones, nearly empty.
A set of picks — not lockpicks, for the record, just dental picks — in a small leather case.
A pair of disposable gloves, folded and carried in a coat pocket.
A map of a building, printed from a browser, with a room circled in red pen.
A voicemail they've listened to eleven times and not deleted, from a number they haven't called back.
Three different receipts from three different coffee shops, all on the same morning, each about twenty minutes apart.
A child's barrette, carried in a breast pocket, not explained.
A notebook full of shorthand that is legible only to the person who wrote it, and possibly not even to them.
A folded twenty-dollar bill kept separate from the wallet — emergency cash, habit, or something else.
A printed itinerary for travel that already happened or hasn't been confirmed yet.
A permanent marker and a specific, private reason for carrying one.
A power bank with a piece of electrical tape over the brand name.
A slim notebook used for logging — times, locations, brief descriptions — going back several weeks.
A small velvet bag containing something hard and angular that they decline to explain.
A hand-drawn map of a neighborhood with three locations marked, and a fourth crossed out.
A folded copy of a will. Their own. Amended recently.
A worn leather card case with nothing in it.
A screenshot printed on regular paper, slightly blurry, of a conversation between two people they know.
A strip of three photo-booth pictures. Two people. One of them is not in the pictures with them anymore.
A cheap pen from a specific hotel. The hotel is across the country from where they say they've been.
A small bottle of hand sanitizer, half empty, and a tic-tac box with something inside that isn't tic-tacs.
An old loyalty card from a business that closed years ago, kept anyway.
A phone charger adapter for a foreign outlet standard.
A medical bracelet worn around the wrist, turned so the engraving faces inward.
A folded printed email, several pages, from a legal firm, about a matter that is clearly ongoing.
A thumb drive in a clear plastic case with a strip of masking tape labeled 'DO NOT OPEN.' The tape is on the outside of the case.
A tube of lip balm that isn't lip balm — the cap unscrews to reveal a small hollow interior.
A set of zip ties — cable management, probably. Probably.
A single key on a paper tag with a four-digit number written in pencil.
A list of questions someone has been planning to ask and hasn't asked yet.
A business card with a name and number, with a different number written over the printed one.
A pair of reading glasses for a prescription that doesn't match their eyes.
A cassette tape in a cracked case. The label says a band name that doesn't exist.
A phone with seventeen unread voicemails and the voicemail icon ignored.
A wristwatch that runs five minutes fast and has done so for years. They know. They don't change it.
A paper clip bent into a specific shape and kept in a front pocket as a fidget object.
A cheap disposable lighter that they don't smoke, carried anyway.
A scribbled note that reads only: 'Not yet. Wait.'
An old library book, overdue by enough that the fine would be more than the book is worth.
A transit day pass stamped with a date from a city that has no transit system.
A sealed envelope addressed to a name and an address that doesn't exist. Written in their hand.
A multi-tool with several functions well-worn and one function that's never been opened.
A single dog tag — military issue — for someone else.
A paper bag with a receipt from a pharmacy inside. The items on the receipt are crossed out in pen.
Three credit cards from three different banks, each in a different name.
A folded printout of a flight manifest. Their name isn't on it.
A small compass — analog, not phone — kept in a shirt pocket.
A folded sheet of graph paper with something that might be architectural plans for a residential building.
A bottle of vitamins that, when opened, contains only two vitamins and a rolled piece of paper.
A paper wristband from a hospital. Not theirs. Dated last week.
A small laminated card with a set of instructions on one side and an emergency number on the other.
A receipt from a pawn shop — for something sold, not bought — from three days ago.
A printed email from a government agency, regarding a matter of ongoing review.
A phone with the camera roll deleted and the recently deleted folder empty.
A roll of cash held with a rubber band, with a name written on the rubber band.
A keychain with no keys on it — just a fob, a rabbit's foot, and a broken carabiner.
A slip of paper with a combination lock number on it. The paper is laminated.
A business card for a person who is deceased. Carried face-down.
A handwritten note that reads 'I know what you did' with no other context, tucked into a coat lining.
A small, cheap digital recorder with forty minutes of audio on it that they haven't described.
A passport in their name, current, for a nationality that is not what they claimed.
A folded, handwritten letter from someone who is dead, addressed to someone who is not them, never delivered.
A phone with a single note open on the screen: a list of names, seven of them, two crossed out, five not.
